GLOSSARY.def: Raw, unroasted coffee beans. They're actually the seed of a fruit (the coffee cherry). Green beans are dense, grassy-smelling, and hold about 10 to 12 percent water. They taste like nothing you'd want to drink until they're roasted.
The moment you drop the green beans into the hot roaster to start the roast. "Charge temperature" is how hot the roaster is at that instant.

Right after charge, the cold beans pull heat out of the roaster, so the temperature reading drops for a bit, then bottoms out and starts climbing again. That lowest point is the turning point. From here the beans are heating up for the rest of the roast.
The first stretch of the roast. The beans shed their moisture and turn from green to yellow. They smell grassy, then like hay, then like baking bread. Usually the longest phase.

The middle stretch, from yellow to first crack. Named after the Maillard reaction, the same browning chemistry that crusts bread and sears a steak. This is where most of coffee's flavor and aroma are built.

An audible popping sound, like popcorn or knuckles cracking, around 196°C / 385°F. Steam and pressure inside the bean rupture its structure and it nearly doubles in size. This is the roaster's most important landmark. No coffee is drinkable if dropped before it, and light roasts are dropped shortly after.

The final phase, measured from first crack to drop. This is where sharp acidity mellows into sweetness and roast character builds. Too short and the coffee tastes grassy and sour; too long and it goes flat, then roasty.

A second, quieter, faster crackle around 224°C / 435°F, more like crisp cereal than popcorn. Oils start migrating to the bean's surface. This is the gateway to dark roasts. Push past it and coffee turns smoky, then bitter, then toward burnt.

The moment you dump the beans out of the roaster into the cooling tray to stop the roast. "Drop temperature" is how hot the beans are at that instant, and it's one of the biggest levers on how light or dark the final roast is. This is the term you'll see most, and it just means: roast's done, get them out and cool them fast.

The temperature of the beans during the roast, read by a probe in the roaster. The main number a roaster watches. Note: absolute readings vary by machine and probe, so roasters care more about the shape of the curve than the exact degrees.

How fast the bean temperature is climbing at any moment, in degrees per interval. It's the slope of the roast curve. A healthy roast has an RoR that smoothly declines from the turning point to the drop. It shouldn't spike up or flatten out.
The graph a roaster lives by: bean temperature plotted over time, usually with the RoR overlaid. Reading the curve is how you steer a roast and how you repeat a good one.

The share of the total roast that happened after first crack, as a percent. If a 12-minute roast hit first crack at 9:36, that's 2:24 of development, or 20 percent. A quick shorthand for how far a roast was pushed. Light roasts run lower (~10 to 15%), darker ones higher.
The industry-standard machine: a rotating metal drum tumbles the beans over a gas flame. Gives coffee body and is stable and forgiving. Most commercial roasting happens on a drum.
A machine that roasts beans on a stream of hot air, like popcorn. Fast, even, and very clean and bright in the cup. Easier to learn on, but less forgiving because it moves quickly.

How much air the roaster pulls through the beans. It does three jobs at once: delivers heat, clears smoke and chaff so they don't taint the flavor, and shapes how fast the beans gain or shed heat. A key control a roaster adjusts throughout.
The papery skin that flakes off the beans as they roast and expand. Roasters clear it with airflow because burnt chaff tastes bad and, in quantity, is a fire hazard.

A numbered scale for measuring roast color, from high numbers (very light) to low numbers (very dark). A standardized way to say how dark a roast is instead of vague words like "medium." Note: the mapping of roast-level names to Agtron numbers varies by region and roaster — this guide uses the prevailing US specialty convention.
How light or dark a coffee is roasted, from light through medium to dark. It's shorthand: two coffees at the same color can still taste different depending on how they got there.

The industry's standard tasting method: brewing coffees the same controlled way and tasting them side by side to evaluate flavor. How roasters judge whether a roast worked and connect a curve to a cup. The modern SCA framework for this is the Coffee Value Assessment (CVA), which replaced the 2004 cupping protocol in November 2024.

Roasting fills the beans with CO2 that escapes over the following days. Fresh-roasted coffee is usually rested at least 12 to 24 hours before brewing so it's not fizzing with gas, which throws off extraction.

Three ways the coffee fruit is processed after picking, before it's shipped as green. Washed is clean and bright, natural is fruity and heavier, honey sits in between. The process changes how the green behaves in the roaster and how it tastes.

The SCA framework that replaced the 2004 cupping protocol in November 2024. Instead of one 100-point score, it evaluates coffee across four separate assessments — physical, descriptive, affective, and extrinsic — for a higher-resolution picture of quality and value.

The key split in the CVA. Descriptive assessment objectively records what a coffee tastes like (intensity, flavor descriptors) with no judgment. Affective assessment records how much a taster likes those attributes. Two cuppers can describe a coffee identically but value it differently.

The 2016 SCA/World Coffee Research reference chart for describing coffee flavor, organized from broad categories at the center (fruity, floral, roasted, nutty/cocoa, etc.) to specific notes at the edge. Used center-outward to build a shared tasting vocabulary.
A professionally certified, calibrated coffee taster — coffee’s equivalent of a sommelier. The program moved from CQI to the SCA in 2025 and is evolving onto the CVA framework.

In cupping, you slurp the coffee off the spoon loudly and forcefully. The hard slurp aerates the liquid and sprays it across the entire palate at once, so you perceive the full range of flavors and aromatics.

During cupping, a crust of grounds floats on top of the steeping coffee. At four minutes you break it with three spoon stirs while smelling — this releases a concentrated burst of aromatics trapped underneath, a key evaluation moment.

curve.name: Reading the Roast Curve

sections.body: A roast curve plots bean temperature (BT) over time. Overlaid on it is the rate of rise (RoR) — the slope of that temperature line, how fast the bean is heating at any moment. Together they are the roaster's dashboard: the BT line tells you where you are, the RoR tells you where you're going.

Every curve has the same anchors: charge (beans hit the drum, temp plunges), turning point (the low, where the bean stops giving up heat and starts absorbing it), drying end (yellowing), first crack, and drop. You read a roast by the spacing and slope between these anchors, not by any single number.

sections.h: Why the shape matters more than the numbers
sections.body: Absolute bean temps are machine-specific — your probe, your drum mass, your placement. What transfers between roasters is the shape: a smooth, continuously declining RoR from turning point to drop. That shape is the closest thing roasting has to a universal quality signal.

keypoints: BT tells you position; RoR tells you trajectory.
Read spacing and slope between landmarks, not absolute temps.
A smooth, declining RoR is the near-universal marker of a clean roast.

refs.label: Scott Rao — roast curve & RoR principles
Coffee roasting (overview)

ror.name: Rate of Rise (RoR)
ror.sub: The slope that predicts the cup

sections.body: RoR is the change in bean temperature over a fixed interval — degrees per 30 seconds or per minute. It's the first derivative of the roast curve. High early (the bean absorbs energy fast), then it should decline steadily as the roast progresses and the temperature gap between drum and bean shrinks.

A healthy roast has a RoR that glides smoothly downward from turning point to drop. It shouldn't rise (a spike overheats the surface), and it shouldn't flat-line (a stall bakes the bean). The single most-watched thing in modern profile roasting is keeping that RoR curve smooth and descending.

Two classic RoR faults cluster around first crack. A crash is a sudden RoR drop as first crack's endothermic reaction pulls energy — the bean momentarily stops climbing. A flick is the RoR rebounding upward right after. Both correlate with muddy, baked-tasting cups. Managing gas and airflow before first crack to prevent the crash is a core roasting skill.

keypoints: RoR = degrees of bean-temp change per interval; the curve's slope.
Target a smooth, continuously declining RoR from turning point to drop.
Prevent the first-crack crash-and-flick by managing energy before the crack, not after.

refs.label: Scott Rao — Development Time Ratio & declining RoR
CoffeeNavigated — RoR, stalling & crash/flick

phases.name: The Phases of a Roast

sections.body: From charge to roughly the point the bean turns yellow. Free and bound moisture (green coffee is ~10–12% water) is driven off. The bean is endothermic and pale. Rushing this phase scorches the surface before the core is ready; dragging it risks a baked, flat roast. Typically the longest single phase.

From yellowing to first crack. The Maillard reaction — amino acids plus reducing sugars — plus caramelization generate hundreds of aroma and flavor compounds and the brown color. This is where most flavor complexity is built. Its length and heat shape sweetness, body, and acidity balance.

From first crack to drop. Also called post-crack development. Here acidity mellows, sweetness rounds, and roast character grows. Too short and the coffee is grassy/sour/underdeveloped; too long and it goes flat then roasty. Measured as a ratio of total time — see DTR.

keypoints: Drying (~10–12% moisture off) → Maillard/browning (flavor built) → development (from first crack to drop).
Maillard is where most flavor complexity forms.
Development balances the trade between underdeveloped (sour/grassy) and overdeveloped (flat/roasty).

refs.label: Curiosity Coffee — phases & Maillard onset (~150°C)
Coffee roasting (overview)

cracks.name: First & Second Crack

sections.body: Around 196–205°C bean temp, the bean fractures audibly — a popcorn-like snap — as built-up steam and CO2 rupture the cell structure and the bean expands. First crack is the single most important landmark: development time is measured from here, and no coffee is drinkable roasted before it. The sound rolls through the batch; you note its start precisely.

Roughly 224–230°C, a quieter, faster, higher-pitched crackle — more like crisp cereal — as the bean's cell walls fracture further and oils migrate to the surface. Second crack marks the transition into dark-roast territory. Roasts landing before it keep origin character; roasts taken into it become roast-forward.

Experienced roasters roast partly by sound. The timing, intensity, and evenness of first crack tell you about the batch's uniformity and moisture. A ragged, drawn-out first crack often signals uneven green or an uneven roast.

keypoints: First crack (~196–205°C): the reference point development time is measured from.
Second crack (~224–230°C): the gateway into dark, roast-forward territory.
Sound — timing and evenness — is a real diagnostic, not just a landmark.

refs.label: MTPak — guide to first & second crack (196°C / 224°C)
Sweet Maria's — First Crack FAQ

dtr.name: Development Time Ratio

sections.body: DTR = (time from first crack to drop) ÷ (total roast time), expressed as a percent. If first crack hits at 9:00 and you drop at 11:15 on an 11:15 roast, development is 2:15 of 11:15 ≈ 20%. It's a normalized way to describe how much of the roast happened after first crack.

Light filter roasts often run 10–15%. The specialty medium sweet spot sits around 18–22%. Darker roasts climb to 25%+ simply because there's more time past first crack. DTR is a guide, not a target to hit blindly — it's meaningful only alongside how the RoR behaved.

DTR is popular because it's a single comparable number, but it can mislead. Two roasts with identical DTR can taste completely different if one crashed and one glided. Use DTR to describe and compare roasts, not to define quality on its own.

keypoints: DTR = (first-crack-to-drop time) ÷ (total time), as a percent.
Rough bands: ~10–15% light, ~18–22% specialty medium, 25%+ dark.
A comparison tool, not a quality guarantee — always read it with RoR behavior.

refs.label: Scott Rao — Development Time Ratio (20–25%)
Daily Coffee News — Rao on development time

chemistry.name: Roasting Chemistry
chemistry.sub: What heat does to the bean

sections.body: The Maillard reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars is the engine of roast flavor, producing melanoidins (brown color, body) and a huge range of aromatics. Strecker degradation, a branch of it, generates key aldehydes behind malty, nutty, and savory notes. Most of the cup's complexity is Maillard chemistry.

Sugars break down under heat independent of amino acids, producing sweet, caramel, and eventually bitter compounds as it goes further. Caramelization and Maillard overlap through browning; how you balance heat and time between them shapes the sweetness-versus-roast character trade.

Chlorogenic acids degrade with roast, producing quinic and caffeic acids that shift perceived acidity and bitterness. Lighter roasts retain more of the bright origin acids; darker roasts break them down, which is why dark roasts read low-acid and bitter.

Roasting generates large volumes of CO2 trapped in the bean, released over days after roast (degassing) — freshly roasted coffee needs rest before brewing. At darker levels, cell walls fracture enough that oils migrate to the surface, the visible sheen on dark roasts.

keypoints: Maillard + Strecker chemistry builds most of the cup's flavor and color.
Caramelization drives sweetness that tips to bitterness if pushed.
Acids degrade with roast — lighter keeps origin brightness, darker turns low-acid/bitter.
Roasting produces CO2 (needs degassing rest) and, when dark, surface oils.

refs.label: Sweet Maria's — roast chemistry & degassing
Coffee roasting (overview)

heat.name: Heat Transfer

sections.body: Conduction is heat from direct contact — bean against hot drum metal. Convection is heat carried by hot air/gas moving through the bean mass. Radiation is infrared energy from hot surfaces. Every roaster uses all three, but the balance between them defines how a machine roasts and tastes.

Convection-dominant roasting (lots of moving hot air) heats evenly and quickly and is easy to control — favored for clean, bright modern profiles. Conduction-heavy roasting (hot drum, less airflow) can build body and a heavier cup but risks scorching if unmanaged. Adjusting airflow is largely adjusting the convection-to-conduction balance in real time.

Airflow does three jobs at once: it delivers convective heat, it clears smoke and chaff (which otherwise taint flavor), and it influences how fast the bean sheds or gains heat. Learning a roaster is largely learning how its airflow interacts with its gas.

keypoints: Conduction (drum contact), convection (hot air), radiation (infrared) — all present, balance defines the machine.
Convection-forward = even and bright; conduction-heavy = body but scorch-prone.
Airflow simultaneously delivers heat, clears smoke, and shapes heat exchange.

refs.label: Perfect Daily Grind — 4 variables that affect roasting

roasters.name: Roaster Types

sections.body: A rotating drum tumbles beans against a heated metal surface with gas heat below and airflow drawn through. The industry workhorse. Strong conductive component gives body; thermal mass makes them stable and forgiving but slower to respond. The default for most commercial specialty and dark roasting.

A stream of hot air both heats and levitates the beans. Almost purely convective: fast, even, very clean and bright in the cup, highly responsive. Popular for sample roasting and light, clarity-focused profiles. Less thermal mass means less forgiving of a mistake — the roast moves quickly.

Many modern machines blur the line: drums with heavily managed airflow, recirculating-air roasters that reuse heat for efficiency and emissions control. The trend is toward more convective control on a drum platform — body of a drum, evenness of air.

keypoints: Drum: conductive body, stable thermal mass, the commercial default.
Fluid-bed: convective, fast, clean and bright, less forgiving.
Modern hybrids add convective control to drum platforms for the best of both.

refs.label: Genuine Origin — roast profiles 101 (roaster types, charge)

defects.name: Roast Defects

sections.body: Dropped too early / too fast past first crack. Grassy, sour, sharp, hay-like; may taste thin. Cause is insufficient development — either too little time or a roast so fast the interior never caught up. The most common defect in beginners chasing light roasts.

The roast lost momentum — RoR crashed or flat-lined — and dragged through the finish. Tastes flat, hollow, papery, lifeless, with muted aroma. Insidious because the color can look correct. Prevented by maintaining a smooth declining RoR and never stalling.

Scorching is surface burn from too much conductive heat / too hot a charge — dark blotches on the bean face. Tipping is small burnt spots at the bean's ends (the tips) from excessive early heat. Both come from delivering energy faster than the bean can absorb it evenly.

Pushed too far — too dark, too far into second crack for the intent. Ashy, carbony, flat, origin character erased. In dark roasts this is a matter of degree; in a roast meant to be bright, even a little is a defect.

keypoints: Underdeveloped: grassy/sour — too little development.
Baked: flat/hollow, correct-looking color — stalled RoR.
Scorched/tipped: surface and tip burns — heat delivered too fast early.
Roasty/over-developed: ashy/flat — pushed too dark for the intent.

refs.label: Scott Rao — baked/underdeveloped & DTR context
Perfect Daily Grind — charge temp, scorching & tipping

profiling.name: Sample Roasting & Profiling
profiling.sub: How a profile actually gets built

sections.body: Before designing a profile you assess the green: density, moisture, screen size, process (washed/natural/honey), and origin. Dense, high-grown, washed coffees take and want more heat; softer, lower-density or natural coffees roast faster and scorch more easily. The green dictates the starting approach.

You roast small sample batches at a few different profiles — vary charge, development time, drop point — then cup them side by side. Cupping is the standardized side-by-side tasting method the industry uses to connect a curve to a flavor. The SCA replaced its long-standing 2004 cupping protocol with the Coffee Value Assessment (CVA) in November 2024, though the legacy 100-point form is still in daily use in many labs. Either way, you're building a map from roast decisions to cup outcomes for this specific coffee.

A profile developed on a sample roaster rarely transfers one-to-one to a production drum — thermal mass and batch size change everything. You re-develop at production scale, using the sample cups as your flavor target, then lock the profile once it's repeatable batch to batch.

Good roasting is disciplined iteration: change one variable, cup the result, keep notes. A repeatable, documented profile that any roaster on the team can hit is the real deliverable — not a single lucky great batch.

keypoints: Assess the green first — density, moisture, and process set the approach.
Sample roast several profiles, then cup side by side to map curve → cup.
Re-develop at production scale using sample cups as the flavor target.
Change one variable at a time, document, and lock a repeatable profile.

refs.label: SCA — Coffee Value Assessment
Liberty Beans — density testing & sample roasting

origin_intro.name: Roasting by Origin

sections.body: A roaster doesn't impose a profile on a coffee so much as respond to it. Density, moisture, screen size, altitude, variety, and processing all arrive baked into the green, and they decide how much heat the bean wants and how it behaves through the roast. The best profile you can build is capped by the green you start with.

Higher-altitude coffees are denser — they grow slower and pack in more sugars and acids. Dense beans need more heat energy to roast through and reward it with complexity, but they also tolerate (and often want) a higher charge temperature with disciplined development. Softer, lower-grown beans have more internal air pockets, transfer heat slower, scorch more easily, and take darker roasts more readily. Many roasters weigh identical volumes of two greens to compare density before designing a profile.

Washed coffees are cleaner and brighter and generally shine light — but a washed coffee hit with too much early energy tends to bake at first crack, over-developing some beans while others stay under. Naturals carry fruit-forward, fermented notes and can spike in temperature before first crack if pushed, inviting defects. Honey sits between. A dense natural Ethiopian and a soft Brazilian natural want opposite charge temps and rates of rise.

Denser / higher-grown / washed → more heat capacity, favors lighter roasts, needs curve discipline. Softer / lower-grown / natural → lower charge temp, slower rate of rise, extend the roast to avoid scorching, takes darker levels well. Everything past that is dialing in the specific lot by cupping.

keypoints: The green's density, moisture, altitude, and processing cap what any roast can achieve.
Dense/high-grown beans want more heat and favor lighter roasts; soft/low-grown beans scorch easily and take dark well.
Washed coffees bake if rushed into first crack; naturals can spike and defect if over-energized.
Weigh equal volumes to compare density before building a profile — then confirm by cupping.

refs.label: Perfect Daily Grind — roasting different origins
Green Coffee Beans Wholesale — roast level & origin

origin_ethiopia.name: Ethiopia

sections.body: Ethiopian specialty grows very high (often 1,800–2,200m), making it dense and demanding. It's also famously variable — thousands of local varieties, mixed screen sizes, and often unknown genetics in a single lot. Expect trial and error to find the profile. The reward is some of the world's finest, most aromatic Arabica: florals, citrus, berry, wine.

Steer away from sudden, intense heat. A hard, energetic start can push out gingery or spice notes but risks torching the delicate florals that define the origin. On washed Ethiopians, too much early energy causes a big energy loss at first crack — baking — which over-develops some beans while others stay under before you have to dump for a light color. On naturals, an aggressive start can spike bean temperature before first crack and invite roast defects.

Light to medium. Washed Ethiopians commonly want a restrained drop with a modest development window and a smoothly declining rate of rise. Too little development gives showy aromatics over a hollow mid-palate; too much turns bright citrus into dull marmalade and wilts the florals. The whole game is preserving the top-end.

keypoints: Very high-grown and dense, but highly variable lot to lot — expect to dial in by trial.
Avoid an aggressive start; it bakes washed lots at first crack and spikes naturals into defects.
Roast light to medium with a restrained drop; the goal is preserving florals and bright acidity.

refs.label: Perfect Daily Grind — Ethiopian coffee: a roaster's guide

origin_colombia.name: Colombia

sections.body: Colombian coffee, especially from regions like Huila, grows at moderate-to-high altitude (roughly 1,200–1,800m) and is typically washed. It's known for balanced acidity, caramel sweetness, and medium body. Medium density makes it versatile and one of the more forgiving origins to roast — a good coffee to learn a new machine on.

Its balance and medium density mean it doesn't punish small mistakes the way a dense Ethiopian or a soft Brazilian can. It takes a standard, well-managed curve well and holds up across roast levels, from a bright-ish medium that keeps its caramel sweetness to a fuller medium-dark for blends and espresso.

Versatile — anywhere from light-medium to medium-dark depending on intent. City+ is a natural home that showcases the caramel-and-balance signature. Push toward Full City for espresso body. Because it's forgiving, it's a strong reference coffee for calibrating a roaster before tackling trickier origins.

keypoints: Moderate-to-high altitude, washed, medium density — balanced and forgiving.
Takes a standard well-managed curve and holds up across roast levels.
A great calibration/reference origin; City+ showcases its caramel-balance signature.

refs.label: One Hundred Coffee — origin & roasting profiles

origin_brazil.name: Brazil

sections.body: Brazilian coffee grows comparatively low (often 800–1,200m, some near 900m), making it softer and lower in density with lower acidity. It's frequently natural-processed and known for nutty, chocolatey, smooth character with natural sweetness and body — which is why it's the backbone of so many espresso blends and commercial coffee.

Because it's soft and low-density, use a lower charge temperature and a slower rate of rise — its air-pocketed structure transfers heat slowly, so a hot, fast start scorches the exterior before the core catches up. Many roasters extend the roast slightly for these coffees. Brazil rewards a touch more development time but demands good curve discipline; flick the rate of rise late and you trade nut-butter creaminess for a drying finish.

Medium to medium-dark. Brazil's sweetness and body caramelize beautifully in that range and stand up to milk, making it a workhorse espresso base. It also takes darker roasts more readily than a dense high-grown coffee. This is a classic candidate for 'stretching' — deliberately extending the Maillard phase to build deeper sweetness.

keypoints: Low-grown, soft, low-density, often natural — nutty/chocolatey with body and low acidity.
Use a lower charge temp and slower rate of rise to avoid scorching; consider extending the roast.
Shines medium to medium-dark; an espresso-blend backbone and a good 'stretching' candidate.

refs.label: Perfect Daily Grind — roasting natural & honey processed
Liberty Beans — 'stretching' the Maillard phase

origin_sumatra.name: Sumatra / Indonesia

sections.body: Indonesian coffees from Sumatra and Sulawesi are known for earthy, spicy, herbal character with full body and low acidity — a signature partly created by the region's distinctive wet-hulled processing. These flavors become more pronounced in darker roasts, which is where the origin is traditionally taken.

Sumatra can handle more aggressive heat application during the middle (Maillard) stage to develop its full body — a head roaster's approach that would obliterate a delicate Ethiopian. Its low acidity and heavy body mean the earthy, herbal signature holds up rather than being lost, so it tolerates and often benefits from a firmer hand than high-grown washed coffees.

Medium-dark to dark. Sumatra's earthy depth and body are traditionally showcased at Full City and beyond, where the roast character complements rather than erases the origin. It's a natural fit for darker blends and for drinkers who want a heavy, low-acid, brooding cup.

keypoints: Earthy, herbal, heavy-bodied, low-acid — a signature of wet-hulled Indonesian processing.
Tolerates firmer heat in the Maillard stage to build body; won't lose its character the way delicate origins do.
Traditionally taken medium-dark to dark, where roast character complements the earthy depth.

refs.label: God of Beans — origin characteristics & density

cupping_intro.name: What Cupping Is

sections.body: Cupping is the standardized way the coffee industry tastes and evaluates coffee. Every cup is brewed identically — same dose, same water, same time — so differences you taste come from the coffee, not the brewing. It's how green buyers judge lots, how roasters check whether a roast worked, and how competitions are scored. If you want to evaluate coffee the way professionals do, cupping is the method.

For a roaster, cupping closes the loop. You design a profile, roast it, then cup it to find out if the curve produced the cup you wanted. Cupping side by side — this profile against that one, this batch against a reference — is how a curve decision becomes a flavor judgment. Without cupping, you're roasting blind.

The reference framework changed recently. In November 2024 the SCA adopted the Coffee Value Assessment (CVA) as its official standard, replacing the 2004 cupping protocol and its single 100-point score that had defined specialty coffee for two decades. Many labs still run the legacy form daily, so working professionals need to understand both — which is why this section covers each.

keypoints: Cupping = brewing every cup identically so differences reflect the coffee, not the brew.
For roasters it closes the loop: curve → roast → cup → judgment.
The SCA replaced the 2004 protocol with the CVA in Nov 2024; both are still in use.

refs.label: SCA — Coffee Value Assessment
Green Coffee Collective — cupping protocol

cupping_mechanics.name: Cupping Mechanics
cupping_mechanics.sub: The physical protocol, step by step

sections.body: The SCA standard is 8.25g of coffee per 150ml of water — a ratio of about 1:18. Measure to 0.1g precision and adjust the coffee weight to your actual cup volume to hold the ratio. Water is clean and 93°C (200°F). Prepare at least 5 cups per coffee so you can judge uniformity across the lot.

Grind coarse — slightly coarser than paper-filter drip, spec'd as 70–75% of grounds passing a US #20 sieve (about 850µm). Grind immediately before pouring; aromatics escape within minutes. Purge the grinder with 5–10g of the next sample first to avoid cross-contamination. Within 15 minutes, smell the dry grounds and note the fragrance.

Pour 93°C water directly over the grounds to saturate them all, and start a 4-minute timer. A crust of grounds forms on top; smell the wet aroma as it steeps. At 4 minutes, break the crust by stirring three times with a spoon while putting your nose close — the crust-break releases a burst of trapped aromatics and is a critical moment. Rinse the spoon between cups. Then skim the foam and floating grounds off the surface in one clean motion.

Begin tasting once the coffee cools to roughly 71°C (around 8–10 minutes in). Load the spoon and slurp hard — the loud slurp aerates the liquid and sprays it across your whole palate. Evaluate flavor, aftertaste, acidity, sweetness, and mouthfeel, and keep tasting as the cup cools, because different attributes reveal themselves at different temperatures.

keypoints: 8.25g : 150ml (~1:18), water 93°C / 200°F, 5 cups per coffee for uniformity.
Grind coarse (70–75% through a #20 sieve), fresh; smell dry fragrance first.
4-minute steep, then break the crust with 3 stirs while smelling; skim.
Taste ~71°C by slurping hard to aerate; re-taste as the cup cools.

refs.label: SCA Protocols & Best Practices (PDF)
Royal Coffee — how to run a cupping
Podium Coffee Club — SCA protocol step by step

cva_overview.name: The Coffee Value Assessment

sections.body: For two decades, coffee was scored on one 100-point number: 80+ meant specialty, and higher was better. The problem is that a single score tells you a coffee is 'very good' but not why, what it tastes like, or whether it suits your market. The CVA, adopted by the SCA in November 2024, replaces that one number with a high-resolution picture across four separate assessments — because describing a coffee and judging how much you like it are different questions that shouldn't be collapsed together.

The CVA has four components, kept deliberately separate to reduce bias. Physical (CVA 102 territory) covers measurable facts like defect counts and moisture. Descriptive (103) objectively describes what the coffee smells and tastes like, using intensity ratings and flavor-wheel descriptors, with no quality judgment. Affective (104) records how much you value those attributes on a preference scale. Extrinsic (105) captures non-sensory value: origin story, traceability, certifications.

sections.h: Descriptive vs affective — the key split
sections.body: This separation is the heart of the CVA. Descriptive asks 'what is in the cup?' — a group of cuppers might all agree a coffee is floral and bright. Affective asks 'do I value that?' — and here they can disagree completely. A Nordic roaster might prize a tea-like acidity that an Italian espresso blender finds too sharp. The CVA records both as valid: the description stays objective, the preference reflects each taster's context.

The descriptive form has no total score by design. Only the affective form produces a number, via a calculator that converts the preference ratings to a 0–100 scale for buyers who still need a shorthand during the transition. This is a real shift in thinking: the CVA treats a coffee's value as multi-dimensional, not reducible to one figure.

keypoints: CVA (Nov 2024) replaced the single 100-point score with four separate assessments.
Physical, Descriptive, Affective, Extrinsic — kept apart to reduce bias.
Descriptive = objective 'what's in the cup'; Affective = subjective 'how much I value it'.
Only the affective form yields a score, converted to 0–100 via calculator.

refs.label: SCA — adopts CVA as cupping standard
Ethio Coffee — CVA guide for buyers
SCA — CVA in Action: Affective Assessment

cva_scoring.name: CVA Affective Scoring

sections.body: The affective assessment scores eight sections for impression of quality: fragrance, aroma, flavor, aftertaste, acidity, sweetness, mouthfeel, and overall. Each is rated on a 9-point hedonic (liking) scale. The 9-point scale is a sensory-science standard because, unlike a 5- or 10-point scale, it has a true neutral midpoint. You're rating preference here, not intensity — 'how much do I like this acidity,' not 'how strong is it.'

The eight section scores are summed, then converted to a familiar 0–100 figure by a fixed formula: Cup Score = 0.65625 × (sum of the eight scores) + 52.75. From that, subtract 2 points for each non-uniform cup and 4 points for each defective cup in the set. The constants are chosen so the output lands in the range specialty cuppers are used to reading.

Say a coffee scores fragrance 7, aroma 7, flavor 6, aftertaste 5, acidity 4, sweetness 6, mouthfeel 7, overall 6. The sum is 48. Then 0.65625 × 48 + 52.75 = 84.25. With no non-uniform or defective cups, the final score is 84.25 — comfortably specialty. This app's digital cupping tool (in the Tools section, when built) runs exactly this math.

One caution echoing the old form: the 0–100 output is a translation for convenience, not the point of the CVA. Two cuppers can land on the same 84 for very different reasons, which is exactly why the descriptive assessment exists alongside it. Use the score as shorthand, and the description for the real picture.

keypoints: Eight sections (fragrance, aroma, flavor, aftertaste, acidity, sweetness, mouthfeel, overall), each 1–9.
Cup Score = 0.65625 × (sum of 8) + 52.75, minus 2/non-uniform cup and 4/defective cup.
Worked example: sum 48 → 84.25, a specialty score.
The number is a convenience translation; the descriptive assessment carries the real detail.

refs.label: SCA — CVA Affective Score Calculator
Royal NY — how to use the CVA form (worked example)
Royal Coffee — what the numbers mean

legacy_protocol.name: The Legacy 2004 Protocol

sections.body: The 2004 SCA (then SCAA) cupping form defined specialty coffee for twenty years, and it's still found in hundreds of roasteries and cupping labs today. Even with the CVA now official, a working professional needs to read and speak the legacy form, because most existing coffee documentation, scoresheets, and habits are built on it.

The legacy form rates ten attributes — fragrance/aroma, flavor, aftertaste, acidity, body, balance, uniformity, clean cup, sweetness, and overall. Most attributes are scored from 6.00 to 10.00 in 0.25-point steps, reflecting quality. The individual scores are summed into a single total. A total of 80+ is the specialty threshold, with higher tiers above it — a coffee in the 90s is exceptional.

Defects subtract from the total. A taint (a minor off-note, usually in aroma or flavor) costs 2 points; a fault (a strong negative that can make the coffee unpleasant) costs 4. In the CVA, this thinking moved to the physical form and the uniform/defective-cup deductions.

sections.h: Legacy vs CVA at a glance
sections.body: The big difference: the legacy form blends description and quality judgment into one number, while the CVA splits them apart. The legacy form asks 'how good is this acidity?' on one scale; the CVA asks 'how intense is it?' (descriptive) and 'how much do I like it?' (affective) as two separate questions. The legacy 'body' also became the broader 'mouthfeel' in the CVA.

keypoints: The 2004 form defined specialty for 20 years and is still in daily use.
Ten attributes scored 6.00–10.00 in 0.25 steps, summed; 80+ = specialty.
Taint = −2, Fault = −4 for defects.
Legacy blends description and quality into one score; the CVA splits them.

refs.label: Femobook — cupping protocols & scoring
Daily Coffee News — SCA adopts new forms

flavor_wheel.name: The Flavor Wheel

sections.body: The Coffee Taster's Flavor Wheel, released by the SCA and World Coffee Research in 2016 (built on WCR's Sensory Lexicon), is the industry's reference for describing what coffee tastes and smells like. It maps hundreds of descriptors in a circle, organized from broad categories at the center to specific references at the outer edge. It's descriptive, not prescriptive — it helps you articulate what you taste without judging quality.

The center holds nine broad categories: floral, fruity, sour/fermented, green/vegetative, other, roasted, spices, nutty/cocoa, and sweet. Each branches outward into sub-groups and then specific notes. Fruity opens to berry, dried fruit, citrus, other fruit; berry opens further to blackberry, raspberry, blueberry, strawberry. The progressive narrowing is what makes it usable.

Work from the center outward. Start with the broadest impression — is this coffee fruity, nutty, roasted, floral? Once you've picked a core category, move out one ring to narrow it: fruity → berry. Then out again to the specific note: berry → blueberry. You can stop at any ring; the further out you go, the more precise the description. This is far easier than trying to jump straight to 'blueberry' from a blank page.

The wheel gives cuppers a shared, calibrated vocabulary. When one taster says 'blackberry' and another says 'jammy dark fruit,' the wheel lets them locate a common reference instead of talking past each other. In the CVA descriptive assessment, the flavor-wheel categories are exactly the descriptors you check off — so the wheel and the modern form are built from the same map.

keypoints: 2016 SCA/WCR wheel: hundreds of descriptors, broad center → specific edge.
Nine center categories: floral, fruity, sour/fermented, green/vegetative, other, roasted, spices, nutty/cocoa, sweet.
Work center-outward, stopping at whatever precision you can honestly taste.
Descriptive not prescriptive; it's the vocabulary behind the CVA descriptive form.

refs.label: SCA/WCR Coffee Taster's Flavor Wheel (PDF)
Green Coffee Collective — the flavour wheel
Not Bad Coffee — interactive flavor wheel

qc_loop.name: The Roaster's QC Loop

sections.body: Cupping isn't only for buying green — it's how a roastery holds quality steady. The discipline is simple: cup your production batches, not just your samples. A profile that cupped beautifully as a sample roast can drift at production scale, and the only way you catch it is by tasting what actually went in the bag.

The powerful move is comparative. Cup a new batch next to a retained reference of how that coffee should taste — your approved profile, or a saved sample. Side by side, drift is obvious that would be invisible cup-by-cup. This is the production version of the sample-roasting loop: the sample cup becomes the target you're checking each batch against.

To check whether two batches are truly the same, cuppers use triangulation: three cups, two of one batch and one of the other, arranged blind. If you can reliably pick the odd one out, there's a real difference; if you can't, they're effectively identical. It's a rigorous, bias-resistant way to answer 'did this batch match the last one?'

sections.h: Close the loop with the curve
sections.body: When a cupping flags a problem, the fix lives back in the roast data. A baked, flat cup points at a stalled rate of rise; a sharp, grassy cup points at under-development. Cupping tells you what's wrong in the cup; the roast curve tells you where to fix it. A roastery that cups its production and reads its curves together has a real quality system, not just good intentions.

keypoints: Cup production batches, not just samples — profiles drift at scale.
Cup against a retained reference; side-by-side makes drift obvious.
Triangulation (2 of one, 1 of the other, blind) tests whether batches truly match.
Cupping finds the flaw; the roast curve tells you where to fix it.

refs.label: Green Coffee Collective — cupping protocol & QC
Scott Rao — development & the cup

q_grader.name: Q Graders & the Evolved Q

sections.body: A Q Grader is a professionally certified coffee taster — the closest thing coffee has to a sommelier credential. Administered for years by the Coffee Quality Institute (CQI), the Q program trained and calibrated graders worldwide to score green coffee reliably on the 100-point scale, so a score in one country meant the same thing in another. There are roughly 10,000 certified Q Graders globally.

In April 2025 the SCA took over management of the Q program from CQI, and announced that an 'evolved Q' credential will be based on the CVA rather than the legacy 100-point form. This is a significant shift: the standard that certifies the world's professional coffee tasters is moving onto the same CVA framework that replaced the cupping form. A transitional 'CVA for Cuppers' course is bridging existing graders across, and is scheduled to retire on November 30, 2026.

For a roaster, you don't need to be a Q Grader to cup well or run quality control — the protocol is open and learnable. But the credential still signals calibrated, trusted palates for buying and competition, and understanding where it's headed (CVA-based) tells you which framework to invest your own training in. This is a live, moving area — check current SCA announcements for the latest status.

keypoints: Q Grader = certified, calibrated professional coffee taster; ~10,000 worldwide.
SCA took over the Q program from CQI in April 2025; the 'evolved Q' is CVA-based.
The transitional 'CVA for Cuppers' course retires Nov 30, 2026.
You don't need the credential to cup well — the protocol is open — but it signals a trusted palate.

refs.label: Daily Coffee News — SCA takes over Q program
Perfect Daily Grind — CVA & the Q programme
